title: Fortinet FortiGate FG-60F Review A Bigger Gateway Firewall
gist: Hands-on teardown of Fortinet's FortiGate FG-60F fanless desktop firewall, positioned as a slightly larger sibling to the FG-40F for edge deployments, with ten Ethernet ports and physical-security features aimed at branch/edge sites.

insights.claim: The Fortinet FortiGate FG-60F is a fanless white desktop firewall closely related to the FG-40F, tested back-to-back with it, indicating Fortinet segments its small-form-factor gateway line into tightly overlapping SKUs differentiated mainly by throughput tier.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Signals Fortinet's strategy of ladder-pricing near-identical edge appliances; buyers should scrutinize throughput/license tiers rather than hardware differences.
insights.locator: A few weeks ago, we published

insights.claim: The FG-60F ships with ten total Ethernet ports pre-labeled as WAN1, WAN2, DMZ, two FortiLink ports, and five LAN ports, reflecting Fortinet's Security Fabric switch-management (FortiLink) push at the branch edge.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: FortiLink ports lock branches into Fortinet-managed switches, extending vendor stickiness beyond the firewall itself.
insights.locator: Next, there are labeled WAN2, WAN1, and DMZ

insights.claim: The FG-60F uses a latching 12V DC power input and includes a Kensington lock point, design choices (shared with SonicWall) that treat physical theft and cable dislodging as real edge-deployment threats.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Physical-security detailing is a differentiator for distributed/retail edge fleets where devices sit in unsecured locations.
insights.quote: For a smaller form factor like this, designed for edge deployments, physical security is a thing even at the level of keeping the box in the building.
insights.locator: On the other side, there is a vent with a lock point

insights.claim: A firmware update on the review unit got stuck and required console-port recovery to interrupt the boot, a reliability friction point for a device meant to be deployed unattended at remote sites.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Firmware-update fragility raises operational cost for large branch fleets lacking on-site technical staff.
insights.locator: There is also a USB and a console port
